channel.title: NPR Topics: News
channel.description: NPR news, audio, and podcasts. Coverage of breaking stories, national and world news, politics, business, science, technology, and extended coverage of major national and world events.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: What will it take to get a vaccine for the Ebola strain driving the current outbreak?
item.description: There is an effective vaccine for Ebola — but not for the variety spreading rapidly in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Trials are going on for several candidates. How long will it take?
item.content:encoded:
There is an effective vaccine for Ebola — but not for the variety spreading rapidly in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Trials are going on for several candidates. How long will it take?

(Image credit: CArol Valade/AFP)
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Lambert

item.title: Gun control group sues ATF over records release
item.description: Brady, a nonprofit gun control advocacy group, is suing the ATF and the DOJ over their refusals to release documents and other information about who the largest sellers of crime guns in the U.S. are.
item.content:encoded:
Brady, a nonprofit gun control advocacy group, is suing the ATF and the DOJ over their refusals to release documents and other information about who the largest sellers of crime guns in the U.S. are.

(Image credit: Charly Triballeau)
item.dc:creator: Jaclyn Diaz

item.title: Israel and Lebanon reach an agreement, but ceasefire stalls
item.description: The U.N. peacekeeping mission for Lebanon, known as UNIFIL, said one peacekeeper was killed and others were wounded when they came under mortar fire in southeastern Lebanon.
item.content:encoded:
The U.N. peacekeeping mission for Lebanon, known as UNIFIL, said one peacekeeper was killed and others were wounded when they came under mortar fire in southeastern Lebanon.

(Image credit: Jalaa Marey)
item.dc:creator: Jane Arraf

item.title: Senate to start debate on ICE funding. And, Israel and Lebanon agree to ceasefire
item.description: Senate Republicans are moving forward on a bill to fund immigration enforcement through the end of Trump's term. And, Israel and Lebanon have agreed to a ceasefire that could aid in ending the war in Iran.
item.content:encoded:
Senate Republicans are moving forward on a bill to fund immigration enforcement through the end of Trump's term. And, Israel and Lebanon have agreed to a ceasefire that could aid in ending the war in Iran.

(Image credit: Anna Moneymaker)
item.dc:creator: Brittney Melton

item.title: More than 1 in 3 World Cup matches face dangerous heat risk, NPR analysis finds
item.description: Dangerously hot, humid weather is likely at many of the 2026 World Cup soccer venues. We crunched the numbers to see which matches are most at risk.
item.content:encoded:
Dangerously hot, humid weather is likely at many of the 2026 World Cup soccer venues. We crunched the numbers to see which matches are most at risk.

(Image credit: Ed Zurga)
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Hersher

item.title: In photos: a preview of the Obama Presidential Center
item.description: The Obama Presidential Center opens later this month in Chicago. We take a look inside.
item.content:encoded:
The Obama Presidential Center opens later this month in Chicago. We take a look inside.

(Image credit: Lucy Hewett for NPR)
item.dc:creator: NPR Staff

item.title: How having zero points in tennis — or 'love' — came to sound so sweet
item.description: There are theories that "love" in the tennis context has French, English or Dutch origins. But like many words, historians and language experts say it's hard to pin down the "right" answer.
item.content:encoded:
There are theories that "love" in the tennis context has French, English or Dutch origins. But like many words, historians and language experts say it's hard to pin down the "right" answer.

(Image credit: Kirsty Wigglesworth)
item.dc:creator: Ayana Archie

item.title: National Guard has done little to reduce violent crime in D.C., a new study finds
item.description: A new study has found that the National Guard's presence in Washington, D.C. had no effect on violent crime in the city. The Guard has been deployed since last August as part of a federal task force to fight crime, and their numbers are set to double in the coming weeks.
item.content:encoded:
A new study has found that the National Guard's presence in Washington, D.C. had no effect on violent crime in the city. The Guard has been deployed since last August as part of a federal task force to fight crime, and their numbers are set to double in the coming weeks.

(Image credit: Alex Wroblewski)
item.dc:creator: Kat Lonsdorf

item.title: How cellphone carriers prepare for hurricane season with AI, drones and 'cows'
item.description: Hurricane season is expected to be milder than usual this year. But that's not stopping cell phone companies from pulling out all the stops.
item.content:encoded:
Hurricane season is expected to be milder than usual this year. But that's not stopping cell phone companies from pulling out all the stops.

(Image credit: Melissa Sue Gerrits)
item.dc:creator: Ayana Archie

item.title: A handful of American households pay for AI. Is the future free — or a subscription?
item.description: Just 3% of U.S. households pay for AI for personal use. Sign ups are growing — even though Americans have subscription fatigue.
item.content:encoded:
Just 3% of U.S. households pay for AI for personal use. Sign ups are growing — even though Americans have subscription fatigue.

(Image credit: CHARLY TRIBALLEAU)
item.dc:creator: Stephan Bisaha
